story:
I gripped the podium and looked out at the room, everyone dressed up—well, most of them; a few of my grandkids forgot the memo, but that was alright. The lights were hot, my collar too tight, and the hardest part was starting. I’d been rehearsing for days. I took out the faded paper I’d kept in my dresser for 63 years. Becca’s list. Three things I had to do.

Number one, wear a nice tux—she’d written “nice” in big, underlined letters. Best $200 I ever spent. Number two, be at the church at 3 p.m. The wedding was actually at 4, but she knew I’d be late, so she told me an hour early so I’d arrive on time. Number three, don’t forget it’s your day too. Love, Becca. I had to pause there. I’d promised myself I wouldn’t get choked up yet, but promises don’t stand up well against memories.

We each had five in our bridal party, and my mother insisted on walking me down the aisle—she threatened Becca more than once about wearing white, but thankfully she didn’t. The bridesmaids wore mint green. I heard a groan from the crowd, and I could see Brandy, Becca’s best friend, trying not to smile. My tie was mint green to match. I sweated more at the altar than I did when I asked Becca to marry me—Joe was there; he remembers.

When the wedding march started and everyone stood, I froze. Couldn’t turn around. My knees went to jelly. The priest leaned over and whispered, “Turn around or you’re gonna miss it.” When I did, it was like a scene out of a movie. The light caught the crystals in Becca’s tiara and they looked like stars. Her veil was long with white embroidery at the edges, her dress had sleeves with white flowers, and she was beaming. The photographer caught that smile. That photo was always my favorite.

Sixty-three years later, here we are. I finally looked at her. She was lying in the casket in a mint green dress, paler than usual, but still herself. I’d seen friends who didn’t look like themselves at the end; Becca looked like she was resting.

I told the room a few things that kept us together. We didn’t agree on everything, but we never fought to win. No one wins an argument; it only leaves hurt feelings. We trusted each other. She knew my phone’s passcode—mostly because I couldn’t remember it. We were best friends, and we also gave each other space. I didn’t go to her book club, and she didn’t come golfing. And we kept date nights going, even after she was in the hospital. I’d said everything I needed to say to her; she knew how much she meant to me, and I knew what I meant to her.

When I sat down, I noticed the flowers. Brandy had sent a big circle of mint green dahlias. Becca would have loved them. After the priest spoke, we stood to take her to her resting place. I took the cold brass handle behind our oldest, Matthew. Some of her former students carried the other side. The day was sunny and warm, not at all how I felt inside. We tossed small handfuls of dirt onto the casket. The plot next to hers is mine. Maddy tugged my arm. Time to go. I turned once more to read the stone: Rebecca Anne Greene, Devoted Wife, Loving Mother, Caring Friend. June 10, 1940 – August 22, 2025.
